summary: Well-structured product-ideal contract separating CDK/Auth external dependencies from internal manager implementation. Clear system/entity/contract taxonomy, explicit NonContract boundary, and strong causal provisioning flows. Two copy-paste duplications (in defines and dashboardIssuer.means blocks) are the main quality defect. Concrete paths, bootstrap fields, and Cloudflare tunnel provisioning detail suggest active operational use.
strengths: Explicit contract/non-contract boundary with named exclusions and coupling-detection when-clauses
Provisioning and auth flows expressed as clear causal chains covering CDK→setup→services and Auth→discovery→issuer→URL
Locally self-sufficient: a reader recovers the full CDK-manager interface contract without needing any companion file
Bootstrap context field list is precise and actionable, covering all CDK-injected values
Prescribes section encodes real architectural guidance (runtime fetch over user-data, entrypoints over wrappers) with low interpretive slack
Named tunnel provisioning contract is unusually thorough, covering deploy-machine prep, AWS secret/parameter patterns, and runtime fetch semantics
weaknesses: Duplicated bullet in defines block: 'If named tunnel configuration is present in runtime config...' appears twice with only whitespace difference
Duplicated bullet in dashboardIssuer.means: 'may reuse one configured named cloudflared tunnel...' appears twice with only whitespace difference
Some .means() blocks mix contract-level semantics with implementation hints (e.g., 'local port 3000') that could drift without being the kind of thing an ideal contract normally pins
The relationship between namedTunnelConfig and namedTunnelProvisioning could be clearer—both define related Cloudflare tunnel concerns but their boundary is implicit rather than causally linked
